title: 1. Terminal Introduction
definition: The terminal is a text-based interface used to control a computer.
explanation: Instead of clicking icons, you type commands. The system executes them and displays results.
instruction: Identify your logged-in username by executing the whoami command.

title: 2. Locate Directory (pwd)
definition: In Linux, your current location in the directory tree is the working directory.
explanation: The pwd (print working directory) command prints your absolute path from the root directory.
instruction: Check the current directory path in the terminal.

title: 3. List Files (ls)
definition: The ls command lists the files and directories inside your current folder.
explanation: By default, it shows visible files. You will see directories like drafts and welcome.txt.
instruction: List directory contents using the ls command.

title: 4. List Hidden Files (ls -a)
definition: Hidden files in Linux start with a dot (.) and contain configurations.
explanation: Passing -a to ls tells it to list all files, including hidden ones like .bashrc.
instruction: List all contents, including hidden configurations.

title: 5. List Details (ls -l)
definition: Long listing format displays permissions, owner, size, and modification date.
explanation: Using ls -l outputs detailed parameters of files in a table layout.
instruction: Run ls with the -l flag to view detailed file info.

title: 6. Navigating Folders (cd)
definition: The cd command changes your working directory location.
explanation: Specify the directory name to move inside. For example, cd drafts enters the drafts folder.
instruction: Change your active directory to drafts.

title: 7. Go Home (cd ~)
definition: The tilde symbol (~) represents your user's home directory path.
explanation: Running cd with ~ or no arguments instantly returns you to /home/student.
instruction: Navigate back to your home directory.

title: 8. Create Files (touch)
definition: The touch command creates a new empty file.
explanation: If the file already exists, touch updates its last-modified timestamp.
instruction: Create a file named draft.txt in the current directory.

title: 9. Create Folders (mkdir)
definition: The mkdir command creates a new directory folder.
explanation: This allows you to organize files in subfolders. Use mkdir projects to create a folder.

title: 10. Write File (echo & >)
definition: The > operator redirects output to a file, overwriting its contents.
explanation: echo 'text' > file writes 'text' into the file. If the file doesn't exist, it is created.
instruction: Write the string 'Hello DevLab' into draft.txt.

title: 11. Append File (echo & >>)
definition: The >> operator appends content to the end of a file.
explanation: Unlike >, >> keeps the existing content of the file and adds new text below it.

title: 12. View File (cat)
definition: The cat command concatenates and displays the contents of a file.
explanation: It prints the entire file content directly to your terminal standard output.

title: 13. Copy File (cp)
definition: The cp command duplicates files or directories.
explanation: Specify the source file followed by the destination path.

title: 14. Move File (mv)
definition: The mv command moves or renames a file.
explanation: Moving relocates the file, removing it from the original path.
instruction: Move draft.txt into the projects directory.

title: 15. Delete File (rm)
explanation: Warning: files deleted with rm cannot be recovered in Linux!
